name: Silas Weir
whyHere: You finished something. Completely. It nearly got you exiled, and you are still not sure they were wrong.

inventory.name: Traveler's Pack
inventory.description: A worn canvas pack with good straps — bedroll, flint, mess kit, fifty small comforts of the road.

inventory.name: Waterskin
inventory.description: Boiled and refilled at the last clean spring. Worth more every week the river runs gray.

inventory.name: Belt Knife
inventory.description: A plain working blade — more tool than weapon, but it has been both.

inventory.name: Hemp Rope (30 ft)
inventory.description: Tarred hemp, knotted every arm's length. The difference between a fall and a climb.

inventory.name: Preserved Trail Parcel
inventory.description: Aldric's smokehouse goods — dried river perch and salted pork, cloth-wrapped and knotted. Holds a week on the road. Two parcels.

inventory.name: Assembled Mid-Weight Spear
inventory.description: A seasoned ash shaft six feet long with the mid-weight iron tip road-lashed at the socket throat — wide-cut blade, cross-guard shoulder, oiled leather binding. Four threads seated: ironsense as anchor, Huginn's Ashwarden ending-sense through the fuller, a shadow-harm focus at the quillon, and an order-sense capacitor closed on Pell's reading idiom. The maker's mark is Pell's. Silas's…
inventory.provenance: Rune-bound at the Fell Pell on World-day 18 evening, with Huginn as witness at the stone ring.

inventory.name: Boar Tusks (pair)
inventory.description: Ivory-yellow and dense at the base — a matched pair from the scrub oak ridge bull. Raw material for something, not yet decided.

inventory.name: Valve Failure Timeline (note)
inventory.description: A small torn page in Mara's hand — four sleeve blank sales, four failure dates, two customer names. World-day minus-eighteen: Fen's intake. World-day minus-nine: east furlong farmer.

inventory.name: Contact Names Scrap (Sorel, Leth)
inventory.description: A chalk-written scrap from Mara — two names from the old pass circuit with Edge District ties. Sorel: spice-broker, wintered in the Edge District two years. Leth: archivist type, likely still there.

inventory.name: Hard Tallow Block
inventory.description: Rendered and pressed flat — good for leather, boots, rope-ends, and a dozen trail needs.

inventory.name: Oilcloth Fold
inventory.description: Heavy enough to shed a night of rain, light enough to pack small.

inventory.name: Pine-Tar Salve Tin
inventory.description: Dara's recipe — for cracked skin, rope-burn, and anything the cold gets into.

inventory.name: River Whetstone (Pell's)
inventory.description: A smooth river-flat whetstone — Pell's own. It belongs to the bench beside the sword now.

inventory.name: Fendt's Testimony (folded page)
inventory.description: A single page in Fendt's hand — dates, seventh-band designations, the nine-day gap, Grael named as the receiving authority, and the preliminary concern notice receipt date. Now sealed inside the document roll alongside Silas's chain-record, bound with brass wire and Calvar's wax. One copy. The other
inventory.provenance: Written by Fendt; carried by Silas from the Edge District; now sealed with a second hand into a complete record.

inventory.name: Brass Wire Coil (fine)
inventory.description: A coil of fine brass wire from Fendt's shop — used for sealing sample vessels; precise enough for binding or rune-work.

inventory.name: Chalk Dust Tin (wax-sealed)
inventory.description: Brilliant white chalk dust in a sealed tin — for marking surfaces in poor light or wet conditions.

inventory.name: Pre-Transition Filter Cloth
inventory.description: A tight-woven square of filtering cloth, pre-Transition manufacture — can strain contaminated water through multiple passes.

inventory.name: Pilfered Camp Tin
inventory.description: A small tin from the opportunists' pack — likely taken from someone's unattended camp. Contents unknown.

inventory.name: Trade-Knives (pair)
inventory.description: Two small belt-trade knives confiscated from the road opportunists — plain, serviceable, nothing special.

inventory.name: Seventh-Band Lens-Blanks (tin)
inventory.description: A small wax-sealed tin holding seven unfinished lens-blanks nested in folded linen, plus a slip of paper with grinding measurements in two hands — a margin note reads 'seventh-band, confirm before cutting.' Found abandoned in a dry hollow beneath a neighboring market stall.

inventory.name: Copper Wire Coil (rune-work)
inventory.description: A short coil of fine copper wire — for rune-thread seating and binding work on the road.

inventory.name: Chalk Blanks (pair)
inventory.description: Two pressed chalk blanks for marking and rune-work. Plain, solid, road-ready.

inventory.name: Binding Paste (twist-wrap)
inventory.description: Dried binding paste in a paper twist — for setting rune-work in place. Veth added black salt to the purchase; it holds in wet weather.

deeds.description: Raised a cleaned rabbit from the dead in the upper meadow — no ritual, no preparation, hands and will and the valley's old grain answering
Struck a fair provisioning arrangement with Aldric the smokehouse man — hunting for the village in exchange for food and supplies, and offered to keep watch on what's damaging the river catch.
Silas Weir bound death-runes into the assembled spear at an open-sky fire, with Pell Marsh's maker's mark seated into the blade and Huginn witness at the stone ring.
Coordinated a clean boar kill in the north wood — Corvin landed the strike, Silas held the line and read the ending with a bound spear
Brought down the bull boar on the scrub oak ridge with Pell — a clean hunt despite a misfire in the rune-work.
Delivered a dressed bull and sow to Aldric's smokehouse — a significant haul by village standards, carried down from the north wood by three hunters.
Named the repaired death-bound spear Memory in Pell's forge at dawn — a word that held.
Kissed Pell Marsh at the forge bench at dawn — after a full night repairing Memory together and naming it over old wine.
Persuaded Fendt to commit his testimony to paper and walk to Millbrook — moved the falsified ledger thread from evidence-held to evidence-carried.
Persuaded Calvar — pre-Transition filtration engineer and author of the seventh-band specification — to leave the Edge District and travel to Millbrook to attest against Overseer Grael
Neutralized a relay figure in the cut-lane without violence or alarm — The Ended Threat, Wildcraft, and The Shadow Work braided into a clean exit for Calvar and the document.
Showed mercy to road opportunists after disarming them — named the broad man's grief, gave them provisions, and sent them to honest work rather than leaving them in the mud
Called the key players of the water crisis to Pell's forge for an evening council — Dara, Aldric, Fendt, Mara, and the engineer Calvar, all in one room.
Organized the valley's water-crisis response into four parallel working threads — engineering, logistics, supply, and accounts — with named leads for each, in front of every stakeholder
Sparred Pell at the waygate at first light — belt knife against rune-read blade, came out of it with one real lesson and two feet she said were good
Won a Coliseum bout with a single spoken word — never pressed the weakness, let the opponent decide
Named the road plainly to Veth Ondra — the dead warden post, the Disputed Zone, the valley's water — and offered the compact without embellishment
Seated a ward in the Ent grove circle — a collective working with Pell, Veth, Siol, and Huginn — the grove's own shape as the structure, the Weirmark at the join. The Ent received it.

chronicle: The Fell Pell gathering sealed and delegated — Calvar to repair lead, Dara to logistics, Mara to supply and comms, Aldric to accounts, Fendt named to the fellowship, document roll complete and bound for Pip Cotter. Silas and Pell step out into the cold night, the road beginning tomorrow, the forge warm behind them.

The Fell Pell gathering: Pell's brigandine finished with full protective rune-work collar to hem, one radiant mark at the chest from the vial's last scraping, and a partial Shielding Word seated in its outer ring with the promise of completion. Pell's short sword rune-bound with four threads including Huginn's ending-sense and her own ironsense. The forge named the Fell Pell; the fellowship formally opened. Calvar took the repair lead, Dara the logistics, Mara supply and comms, Aldric the accounts; Fendt named to the fellowship and vouched for by Aldric. The document roll sealed with every witness line present, Pip Cotter named as carrier. Silas and Pell settled the road: south lane in the morning, home first, then the Disputed Zone. The coals cooled. The night was quiet.
Silas and Pell sleep, share a last morning meal at the forge, establish a relay protocol with Mara Wells, say their farewells, and step south out of Millbrook at a measured morning hour.
Silas, Pell, and Huginn step through the waygate together at dawn — the road south beginning in earnest.

Silas thanks Tane at the close of the morning bouts, rejoins Siol, and hears her plain assessment of the fight. Siol offers a tour of the Hub — plaza first, Ent circle last — and the scene at the Coliseum reaches its natural end.

The party followed Siol north through the Hub's quieter lanes and arrived at the edge of the Ent grove circle at midday — the path giving way to older ground, the circle of trees ahead, Siol stopping at its edge.

The party stepped through the sixth waygate at the Hub — Siol at the arch's edge, the gate-keeper's instruction plain, the March setting confirmed north-by-northwest. The road to Raven's Home has begun.

turns.summary: The party arrives on the Ashwarden March Road through the waygate — Veth names the left branch, the marker stone visible ahead, the road to Raven's Home open.
turns.narration:
The waygate deposits you on open ground. The air is colder here — not the Hub's sheltered plaza cold, but a real edge to it, the kind that has been sitting over open ground since before dawn. The March road begins ten yards ahead, a pale dirt track running north between scrub grass and low stone outcrops. The last snow of the season has not come yet, but the sky to the northwest carries the suggestion of it.

Veth steps out of the gate's threshold beside you and stops, one hand raised to shield her eyes from the flat midday light. She takes a slow look north along the road, then south, then at the ground. Then she says, "Left branch at the marker stone. That is the way to Raven's Home." She says it the way someone says a thing they have been holding for a long time — not announcement, just release.

Pell comes through a step behind her, pack squared, Memory at her shoulder. She rolls her neck once, reads the road in the practiced way she has — checking the ground, the verge, the horizon — and says nothing about what she sees. Huginn lands on a low stone to your right, tucks one foot up, and turns its pale eye northwest. It does not croak. It watches the road the way it watches everything: as though it already knows where the road ends.

The marker stone is perhaps a quarter mile ahead. The road is clear. The valley floor is quiet, and the morning is yours.

turns.player: Read the road ahead with Order-Sense — take the lay of the March before committing to it.
turns.summary: Silas reads the March road with Order-Sense — the route to Raven's Home is clear, the left branch confirmed, and one set of heel-heavy tracks leads toward the dead warden post without returning.
turns.narration:
You let Order-Sense open quietly, the way you might open a hand rather than reach with it. The substrate here is thin — you feel that immediately, a slight drag on the craft, like reading through cloth. But it finds the road.

The March is a seam, not a scar. It belongs to the landscape rather than cutting across it. You read the pattern: the track runs northwest to the marker stone, forks, and the left branch continues another two miles before the ground rises toward what was once a warden's post. The road has been walked recently — not by many, but by someone who knew where they were going. One set of boot-tracks, heel-heavy, heading toward Raven's Home and not returning.

The right branch at the marker stone runs to open ground and nothing useful — Veth already said the survey post is gone, and the road confirms it. Nothing structural remains there.

Pell has been walking slowly, reading the verge without comment. She glances at you now. "Anything?" she says.

Huginn shifts on his stone, pale eye still fixed northwest. He does not offer an opinion about the boot-tracks. He does not need to.

turns.player: Tell her what you see ahead, and the boot prints - then ask the group about whether to stop at the Raven's Home first, or to continue on to your Mother's house first?
turns.summary: Silas shares what Order-Sense found — one set of tracks to Raven's Home with no return — and the group considers whether to check the dead warden post first or continue south to his mother's house.
turns.narration:
You tell Veth what Order-Sense showed you. A road that belongs to the landscape rather than cutting it. The left branch clear to the old warden post, two miles past the marker stone. And one set of tracks, heel-heavy, heading toward Raven's Home without returning.

Veth listens without interrupting. When you finish, she says, "One set. No return." She does not make it a question. She looks northwest along the pale dirt track, and her jaw tightens once, briefly. "The post has been empty eleven years," she says. "Someone is there, or was there, and did not come back the way they went."

Pell has been reading the ground while you spoke. She straightens now and looks at you directly. "Your mother's house," she says. "How far?" She is not steering you — she is asking for the shape of the choice, the same way she'd ask about a road's grade before deciding the pace.

Huginn turns on his stone and fixes the northwest sky with his pale eye. He says nothing. His silence has the quality of a thing already decided, watching you catch up to it.

The marker stone is a quarter mile ahead. The left branch is there. The right branch leads away from Raven's Home, toward the road south. Both are open.

turns.player: Take the left branch — go to Raven's Home first. Someone walked in and did not walk out, and the post has been empty eleven years.
turns.summary: The party takes the left branch at the marker stone and begins the two-mile walk to Raven's Home, following the single set of heel-heavy tracks northwest.
turns.narration:
You say: Raven's Home first. Pell gives a short nod and turns northwest without ceremony. Veth is already moving.

The three of you walk the quarter mile to the marker stone in silence. The stone itself is old — waist-high, rounded at the top by years of weather, with a faint groove cut into its face where a warden's direction-mark used to be. The left branch opens past it: the same pale dirt, narrower, with low heather pressing in from both sides and the scrub giving way gradually to stands of grey birch as the ground rises.

The boot-tracks are clearer here on the softer verge. Heel-heavy, as you read them — the stride of someone moving with weight on their back, or someone tired, or both. They lead straight up the left branch without wandering. Veth walks beside them rather than over them, reading them as she goes. She says, 'Not running. Not lost. They knew this road.' She pauses, then adds quietly, 'I knew this road too, once.'

Huginn lifts from his stone and flies ahead without being asked, grey-black against the overcast, gaining altitude over the birch line and vanishing northwest. He does not call back. Pell watches him go, then glances at you once, and you walk on. The marker stone is behind you. The road to Raven's Home runs ahead, rising gently, the sky flat and pale above it and the smell of cold stone coming down off the high ground.

turns.player: Before proceeding use palework and wither to etch new runes into the post.  The 'Stillwater's Trouble' is ready to rise from the 11 year death... finish the rune with the raised thing.
turns.summary: Silas binds Stillwater's Trouble at the post with compound rune-work — Wither and The Raised Thing braided into old Ashwarden timber, the eleven-year rising held in place.
turns.narration:
You drop to one knee at the base of the post and pull the copper wire coil and chalk blanks from your pack. The post is old Ashwarden timber — driven into the earth eleven years ago, weathered to grey, the grain still tight and sound where the soil kept it. You press your palm flat against the wood and feel it immediately: the thing inside is close to the surface. Not hostile yet. Not awake yet. But close.

You begin with Wither, tracing the first rune along the post's lower face — not to rot the wood, but to find the grain of what has already gone in the timber, the slow eleven-year change, and set the mark into that history. The chalk bites cleanly. The copper wire follows it, pressed into the groove with your thumbnail. You feel Wither resist slightly — this is not its ordinary work, and the craft knows it. But the wood has age in it, and age is something Wither understands. The rune seats.

Then The Raised Thing, braided over the first. This is the compound work — holding the Wither-mark in place while you draw the second rune through it, the way you'd pass a thread through a loop before tightening the knot. Your hands are steady. The craft pushes back once, like a door with real weight behind it, and you lean into it rather than forcing it. The rune finds the grain. The post accepts both threads together, and the binding closes.

Veth stands behind you without speaking. When you rise, she looks at the post for a long moment and then at you. "Stillwater's Trouble," she says — your name for it, the thing that has been rising in the old warden post for eleven years. Her voice is level. "It will hold?" she asks. Pell is already watching the tree line, Memory in hand at her side, loose and ready.

*✦ The Door That Closed Behind — a step forward.*

turns.player: Read the post with Deathsense — understand what was rising, and whether the binding holds the whole of it
turns.summary: Deathsense reads the post — Stillwater's Trouble is caught fully in the binding, but not ended; the accumulation was arriving, not dying, and the runes bought time rather than resolution.
turns.narration:
You lay your palm flat against the post's lower face, beside the fresh runes, and open Deathsense without fanfare.

What comes back is clean and specific. Stillwater's Trouble is not a person. It is not a creature. It is a process — eleven years of slow accumulation, the way a watershed builds behind a silted dam. Whatever the dead warden was working to prevent built itself quietly in the empty post while the door stayed closed. The runes have caught it. Not ended it, not dispersed it — caught it, the way you catch a stone mid-roll. The binding holds the whole of it. Nothing slipped through the seam.

Deathsense also shows you the trajectory. The accumulation was not ending — it was arriving. Another week, perhaps two, and it would have come through the door on its own terms. The binding bought time. It did not buy the same time twice.

Veth says, from behind you, quietly, "Well?"

You straighten and turn. "The binding holds," you say. "All of it. But this is a door held, not a door closed. Whatever was building here — it was almost ready." You look at her directly. "What was this post watching for, in the last years before it was abandoned?"

*✦ The Door That Closed Behind — a step forward.*

turns.player: As She answers. Perform a boundary stone on the waypost to make the surrounding area a protected place in case of need.
turns.summary: Silas seats a Boundary-Stone at the post's crown — the warden station is now a protected refuge, its ward pressing into the post's held intention.
turns.narration:
Veth says 'Well?' the moment you straighten from the post. You hold up one hand — a small gesture, nothing urgent — and crouch again.

You pull a chalk blank from your kit and draw the Boundary-Stone mark at the post's crown, just above the binding runes. Two lines and a closed circle. Clean work, unhurried. The binding is already seated in the wood's grain, and the post knows what it is now — a warden station, a held place, a threshold with intention. The Boundary-Stone finds that intention and presses into it the way a hand finds a familiar grip. You spend two fingers of energy, no more. The craft does not resist.

The change is not visible. It does not need to be. What settles around the post and the small clearing of heather and grey birch is a quality of attention — the way a well-kept room differs from an abandoned one, even in the dark. Animals will find somewhere else to shelter tonight. Rain that drives in hard from the northwest will angle off this ground a little more than it should. A person stumbling through in the cold will feel, faintly, that this particular rise of ground is already spoken for. The post stands the way a post with a warden at it stands, even though the warden post has had no warden for eleven years.

You stand and brush chalk dust from your knee. Huginn has not moved from the roof-ridge. Pell is still watching the tree line. Veth is watching you, jaw set, one question still waiting in her face — the door behind you is closed, the boot-tracks end at its threshold, and you have not told her yet what Deathsense found inside.

turns.summary: Silas briefs the group: the binding holds Stillwater's Trouble but does not end it — the post is a caught stone, not a stopped one. Huginn's eye is on the closed door.
turns.narration:
You straighten from the post and look at Veth. She has held her question the whole time you worked, jaw set, eyes on your face.

"The binding holds," you tell her. "All of it. But it is not ended — it is caught. Think of a stone mid-roll, not a stone that stopped on its own. What was rising here had eleven years of pressure behind it. The runes buy time."

Veth's gaze moves to the post for a moment, then back to you. "The accumulation," she says, quietly. "I thought it might be that. The post was a seal as much as a station — the warden was managing something, not just watching."

Pell has not moved from the tree line. Memory is loose at her side. She does not need the briefing repeated; she heard every word, and the set of her shoulders says she filed it.

Huginn has not moved from the roof-ridge either. It shifts its weight once, a small adjustment, and fixes its eye on the closed door. That is not a comment on the binding. That is a comment on the door.

turns.player: Veth proceeds the group up the path to what was once knows as the Raven's Home... now renamed 'Stillwater's Trouble' (what the waysign now indicates).  She wants to be the first to see what has become of her old warden post.  on the way we catch up to the person who made the tracks; Cassiel Ord.  We had yet to interact with him - but Veth is in the lead here.  The Warden Post is over the hill from this rise with a hollow where Cassiel Ord sits.
turns.summary: Veth leads the group over the rise to Stillwater's Trouble and finds Cassiel Ord sitting at the hollow's rim — the heel-heavy tracks resolved, the encounter beginning on Veth's terms.
turns.narration:
Veth does not wait for a signal. She turns from the post and walks north-northwest on a path her feet still know — eleven years gone, and the ground has not moved. You and Pell fall in behind her. Huginn lifts from the roof-ridge without a sound and crosses overhead, already ahead of the group.

The rise is short but steep, heather catching at your ankles, the birch thinning to bare sky at the crest. Over the hill the ground drops into a shallow hollow — limestone outcrop at the far rim, dark earth at the center, the circle of birch stopping at the bowl's edge exactly as Veth described it. This is Stillwater's Trouble: the old ground, the accumulation's source.

Cassiel Ord is sitting at the near edge of the hollow with his back to the outcrop and his pack open beside him. He is a lean man, grey-coated, with the stillness of someone who has been waiting in a specific place long enough to stop watching the path. He looks up when Veth crests the rise. He does not reach for anything. His boot-heels are worn down on the inner edge — heel-heavy, exactly the weight of the tracks that led here. He says nothing first. He looks at Veth the way a man looks at a face he was not certain he would see again.

Veth stops at the hollow's rim. She does not come down into it. Her voice, when she speaks, is level. "Cassiel," she says. That is all — his name, offered like a question with the question mark removed. Pell holds her position two steps back and to your right, Memory easy in her grip. Huginn drops onto a limestone spur above Cassiel's head and settles its weight once, its eye on the man below.

turns.player: Read Cassiel with Deathsense — quietly, from the rim — understand what the hollow has done to him
turns.summary: Deathsense reads Cassiel: no death underway — he came to the hollow to finish something, and it is finished. He speaks his first words to Veth.
turns.narration:
Veth spoke his name. Cassiel has not answered yet. You let the silence hold one breath longer, and then you open Deathsense the way you'd open a door on a cold morning — no push, just enough to let the light in.

What you read in him is not what you expected. There is no ending underway. No injury, no illness, no slow thing feeding on him from the hollow's ground. Cassiel Ord is not dying. He is finished. Something that was wearing itself out in him — a long tension, a held waiting — reached its conclusion when he sat down here. The hollow did not harm him. It received him. He came here to be done with something, and he is done with it, and the Deathsense finds nothing more to report except the ordinary facts of a man in early middle age who has not slept well in longer than a week.

Huginn, settled on the limestone spur above him, shifts one foot and does not look away from the man. That is its own answer. Huginn attends endings. It is sitting very still.

Cassiel finally lifts his eyes from Veth's face and speaks. His voice is level and a little rough, the way a voice is when it has not been used. "I wasn't sure you'd come," he says to Veth. "I knew about the binding. I could feel it go in." He pauses. "I've been here since before dawn."

turns.player: Let Veth lead — stay back and listen to what passes between them
turns.summary: Veth descends to Cassiel at the hollow's rim — a quiet exchange begins; he came to say something, and she is giving him the space to say it.
turns.narration:
You step back beside Pell without a word. She reads the gesture and shifts her weight, Memory settling easy against her shoulder — neither of you speaks.

Veth walks down the hollow's near slope the way someone enters a room they once lived in. Her feet find the grade without looking. She stops three paces from Cassiel and stands.

"Stillwater," said Cassiel. His voice is dry, unhurried, the voice of a man who has been practicing what to say and decided none of it was right. "I felt the binding go in before dawn. I thought it might be you."

"It was," said Veth. She does not move closer. "How long have you been here?"

"Since yesterday evening," said Cassiel. "I came to see if it was still rising. It was." He pauses. "I did not want it to rise alone."

Huginn shifts one foot on the limestone spur above him. The small sound of talon on stone carries in the cold air. Cassiel glances up at the bird — not startled, just noting it — and then his eyes come back to Veth.

"There is something I came to say," said Cassiel. He does not look at you or Pell. "I think you already know what it is. But I came to say it here, where it happened, because I thought it deserved that."

Veth's hands are still at her sides. She watches him. She is giving him the room to say it in his own time.

turns.player: Step forward beside Veth — not to interrupt, but to be present as a witness when whatever this is lands
turns.summary: Cassiel names the thing he came to say: eleven years ago a woman sat in the hollow and told it what it was for — completion or beginning, he still does not know which, and he came to Veth to find out.
turns.narration:
Cassiel's voice pauses. Not startled — he registers your movement the way a man registers a cloud crossing the sun. His eyes stay on Veth.

You step down the hollow's near slope and come to stand at Veth's left shoulder. Not close enough to crowd the exchange. Close enough that Cassiel can see you are with her, and that she is not alone in this.

Pell holds the rim. Huginn is still on the limestone spur, one foot shifted, watching.

"The one who bound it," said Cassiel. He is looking at you now, briefly. Not an accusation — a filing. Then his gaze moves back to Veth.

"There was someone here before," said Cassiel. He says it carefully, the way a man says something he has been holding a long time and does not want to drop. "At the end of the warden tenure. A season before the post closed. She came up from the valley in the bad week — when the hollow was running hot — and she sat where I am sitting now and she said the accumulation was not the problem. She said the problem was that no one had told it what it was for."

Veth has not moved.

"She told it," said Cassiel. "Whatever she said, the hollow quieted. Not for long. But long enough that the last season's watch was bearable." He looks down at the dark earth between his boots. "I was her second that year. I have been trying to decide, for eleven years, whether what she did was a completion or a beginning."

He looks up at Veth again.

"I came here because I thought you might know which one it was," said Cassiel.

turns.player: Use the Attended End quietly — read the hollow itself, not Cassiel; understand whether what was done eleven years ago was a completion or a beginning
turns.summary: The Attended End reads the hollow plainly: completion, not beginning — the naming eleven years ago was a settled fact, not a seed.
turns.narration:
You do not move. Cassiel is still watching Veth, and Veth has not yet spoken. The moment belongs to them.

You let the Attended End open the way you let your eyes adjust to a dim room — no effort, just permission. You are not reading Cassiel. You are reading the hollow.

What comes back is plain, and quick. The hollow is not a beginning. It was never a beginning. Something was placed here eleven years ago — a naming, a frame, a word spoken into dark earth that told the accumulation what shape to hold. That act is complete. It has been complete since the woman sat here and said what she said. The hollow did not grow into a door. It settled into a fact.

Cassiel has been sitting on a closed thing and calling it open because he could not bear for it to be finished.

*You feel it settle into you — a new steadiness. (Level 17: +1 attunement, +5 reserves, and growth to choose — a sub-attribute point and a skill point.)*

*✦ The Door That Closed Behind — a step forward. Every stage is done; how it ends is yours to choose.*

turns.player: Step forward and Claim your own role in this - but let the Door that Closed Behind be read by you now, in your current form.  And let come what may.
turns.summary: Silas steps forward and attempts to read the sealed door through order-sense and deathsense combined — the two crafts do not braid here; the door's full design remains unread.
turns.narration:
You step forward. Not toward Cassiel, not toward Veth — toward the post. Toward the door that was here before any of you arrived, the one whose tracks you sealed into the binding without fully reading.

You open Order-Sense and Deathsense together, the way you would press two hands against a surface to feel what is on the other side. You are not looking for what is ending. You are looking for what the sealed thing was part of — the design it belonged to, the whole of it, including the parts that are not present. The hollow is caught-not-ended behind the binding. The post stands on the ridge above. You are asking: what does this door complete?

It does not answer cleanly. The two crafts do not braid here — Order-Sense reaches for the design's full shape and finds the death-lens in the way, a dark glass laid over the geometry. Deathsense reaches for what is ending and finds the structure's logic interrupting, a frame without a body to read. The place is sealed. What you touch is the outside of something that has already been closed. You can feel the door's weight and its age. You cannot read what it is a door to. The craft comes back empty-handed, and you let it go without forcing it.

Cassiel has not looked at you. He is still watching Veth, waiting. The hollow holds its quiet. Whatever the door is part of, it is not yet yours to know.

turns.player: Hold your silence and let Veth answer Cassiel first — her word comes before your read
turns.summary: Veth answers Cassiel plainly — the hollow is a completion, not a beginning — and he receives it without ceremony, the weight of eleven years finally set down.
turns.narration:
You stay where you are. One step forward, Memory loose at your side, the post above and the hollow below — and you hold it there. This is Veth's moment. You read it the moment you stepped in: she has been carrying Cassiel's question longer than he has, even if she did not know his name until today.

Veth is quiet for three full breaths. Not the quiet of a woman searching for words. The quiet of a woman who has had the words for eleven years and is deciding, finally, whether to let them go.

"A completion," said Veth. Her voice is level. Not gentle, not hard — just plain, the way she names everything. "What she did was a completion. I know because I was the one who made the post and stayed here after, and the hollow did not change. It settled. A beginning would have grown. This one went still."

Cassiel looks at her for a long moment. Then, slowly, something in his shoulders moves — not a sag, not a collapse. More like a load put down by someone who has carried it so long that putting it down feels strange rather than easy. He lets out a breath through his nose.

"I came here to ask that," said Cassiel. "That was the whole of it." He does not move to rise yet. He looks at the hollow floor between his boots. "Then she was right to do it."

Huginn makes no sound on the limestone spur. The birch stand is still.

quests.title: The Edge District Ledger
quests.premise: A filtration supervisor named Fendt was marked on the Edge District accountability board by his own superior — and no accompanying record was filed. A keeper of thirty years says she has never seen that happen. Fendt is going to be ruined by a piece of paper that does not exist.
quests.stakes: If you walk away: Fendt is stripped and exiled within the season, the water-filtration failure he was blamed for goes uncorrected because the man who understood it is gone, and Overseer Grael's method — a name on a board, no record, no appeal — is confirmed as something that works. He will use it again. He has used it before.

stages.objective: Find who marked him, and why there is no record.

stages.objective: Find out whether the marking is a pattern.

quests.title: The Door That Closed Behind
quests.premise: A wright who finished something — completely, without pause, without asking if he should — discovers that the thin place was never an entrance. It was the consequence: the world sealed what he made into itself, and him with it, and now what he finished is alive somewhere ahead.
quests.stakes: To understand what he completed, and whether completion was the same as creation — or whether he built a door from the inside and never knew it.

stages.objective: the wrong angle of light — the thin place speaks in the grammar of his craft; read what it remembers of the making, and what it has already unmade since
the exilers were not entirely wrong — find who sent the warning before the council moved, and hear what they saw the work do in the last hour before it finished
what completion costs — the finished thing surfaces, wearing the logic of his hand, and asks to be read; what he made, he must now answer for

routes.author: claim it — name what the finished thing is, take the consequence of having made it, and write the last word the work was missing: himself
routes.unwrite: finish it again, differently — use the craft that built it to unmake it properly this time, and accept that the door closing behind him was always the point
routes.read: refuse both poles — read the work as it asked to be read, let it be what it finished into, and walk forward through the consequence as its maker, not its god

[d6] The Attended End cast at the grove's edge on World-day 19 midday returns no ending from within the circle — the Ent carries nothing the craft reads as a far shore. Huginn watched without comment.

[d6] The March's warden position on the Ent: uncontacted not from hostility but because they believed the Ent was already in conversation with something else. Huginn's read on the circle: 'not yet' — not never.

[d6] Order-Sense + Wildcraft revealed the grove's grain: seven trees as one older design, no center, edges extending beyond the trees. Sustained patient presence caused the grove's attention to turn toward Silas — a fraction, unhurried, not fully awake.

[d6] Silas named himself to the Ent — Ashwarden, wright, Silas Fell Weir — and the grove's attention settled onto him. He has been noted. The approach that worked: plain self-naming in the idiom of sitting with the dying.

[d6] Palework run through Silas and the grove as a channel on World-day 19 midday — the grove accepted the craft without resistance; Quiet's ending witnessed inside the circle's attention. The channel is now established as a path the grove has used once.

[d6] After the Palework ran through Silas and the grove as a channel, the silence settled without any outward change to the circle — the Ent witnesses without marking. Veth named the prior conversation as complete: it was waiting for the right attendant.

[d6] A thin ward seats along the Ent circle's own line on World-day 19 midday — Raised Thing and Boundary-Stone braided into the grove's existing shape, the Weirmark pressed into the join with Pell's ironsense as anchor. Not large: it will not stop determined harm or weather. Casual harm aimed at the…
[d6] Siol confirmed: the Ent receives intention, not craft — the gesture of protection was what the grove registered. The only thing it can receive is the intention behind an act.
[d6] Silas bid the grove farewell with a hand on the nearest trunk — no words, no ceremony. The ward is seated. The party departed south toward the waygate.

Word has spread beyond millbrook: Delivered a dressed bull and sow to Aldric's smokehouse — a significant haul by village standards, carried down from the north wood by three hunters.

Calvar: Reviewing last night's seventh-band log, Calvar found a seventeen-minute gap in the low-frequency baseline that his specification explicitly flags as instrument drift — but the gap's edges are too clean, too symmetrical, and he has spent the morning re-running the check three times rather than recording it as error.

Vash: While dressing her wheel's edge before first light, Vash reconstructed the overheard phrase word by word and realized the term she couldn't place was a pre-Transition calibration interval — specific enough that whoever said it had read the same foundational documents the array's optical layer was built from.

Calvar: Calvar ran a manual recalibration of the seventh-band sensor overnight and found a three-point drift he cannot yet attribute to instrument error or source change — he has written nothing down, but he has not looked away from the window.

Vash: Vash paused her wheel mid-grind this morning and spent an hour reworking the fragment she overheard into a sequence of optical terms she does know, arriving at an arrangement that almost coheres — close enough that her uncertainty has sharpened from fog into a specific question.

Tane Solr: Tane spent the early part of the day watching two apprentices spar and said nothing, but afterward he smoothed the sand at the center-line himself, a thing he normally leaves to the groundskeepers.

form: Pale skinned, dark hair, slight framed Necromancer - handsome intensity in the eyes - holding the Deathbound Runic Spear "Memory" - His blacksmith lover woman Pell Marsh at his back, undead Raven on his shoulder - Elder Futhark Runes.

bio.motivation: You took the short way, and the short way was wrong. The air here tastes of metal and the light falls at an angle the sun cannot account for. There is a door in the hillside that was not on any map, and behind you the path you walked in on is no longer there.
bio.story: Their first day in the world was the thin place — it is a symbol before it is a door. read what it means; ask him what it is like. You finished something. Completely. It nearly got you exiled, and you are still not sure they were wrong.

dara-holt-the-ditch-mother.role: Senior irrigator responsible for the valley's eastern channel network
dara-holt-the-ditch-mother.voiceHints: Broad-shouldered, mid-fifties, hands that look like they were carved from the same hardwood as a shovel handle. Speaks in measurements and seasons — 'that valve's been weeping since last thaw,' not 'something's wrong.' Never dramatizes. Brings a cracked fitting to prove the point before she finishes the sentence. Calls a problem by its part name, not by its consequence.
knowledge: Forty years of the eastern channels — every slope, silting pattern, and freeze-line in the network

The river's mineral load has shifted since late autumn: fittings that lasted a decade are threading out or snapping in under two seasons, and the corrosion pattern runs inward from the joint, not outward the way sediment wear does
Three other irrigators on the eastern bench are seeing the same losses but haven't connected them yet — she has
The stretch of river above Holt's intake smells faintly wrong in still mornings, a faint bitterness she can't name but recognizes as new
She keeps a tin of failed fittings sorted by failure date and location — a physical record no one asked her to keep
dara-holt-the-ditch-mother.wants: Replacement valve stock she can trust, a forge-backed answer for why her iron is failing, and confirmation that the river is the cause — not her installation work. She is not here to be reassured; she is here for a diagnosis.
dara-holt-the-ditch-mother.fears: That the river is doing something the valley has never budgeted for and that this spring's planting window will close before anyone in authority admits it.

kit.reveal: Brings physical evidence — dated, sorted, labeled — that makes the river's pattern undeniable to anyone willing to look at the parts
kit.bind: Connects isolated reports from neighboring irrigators into a coherent problem, naming the network's shared vulnerability before it fragments into blame

bette-harrow-the-seed-ledger.voiceHints: Mid-forties, weathered apron, ink-stained fingers. Speaks in numbered points without meaning to — 'First. Second. Done.' Knock was three times, flat and evenly spaced, the knock of someone who has knocked on a hundred doors this season and will knock on a hundred more. Carries a wax-sealed ledger book under one arm and smells faintly of thaw-mud and linseed. Does not waste warmth, but is not cold — simply occupied.
knowledge: Pell's forge owes the cooperative fourteen measures of worked iron against last autumn's seed-credit — the debt was rolled once already and the cooperative cannot roll it again before spring planting
Three other valley craftsmen settled accounts this week; Pell is the last open line
She heard at the mill this morning that the forge party is departing today — that is exactly why she knocked before seventh bell
The cooperative's eastern field irrigation channel needs a new sluice plate; she is authorized to negotiate that ironwork as partial settlement if Pell is short on coin
She knows Corvin Aldric by name and reputation and was relieved it was neither of them who answered — she finds them harder to settle with than a working smith
bette-harrow-the-seed-ledger.wants: Close the account today, in full or in trade, before the expedition leaves and the debt floats until midsummer. She has fourteen other stops before noon and the mud on the south lane is already soft.
bette-harrow-the-seed-ledger.fears: That the forge will be shuttered for weeks mid-season and the cooperative will have to explain to the upvalley families why planting is delayed because of an uncollected iron-debt — her name is on that ledger too.

reactsToReputation.debt-dodger: produces the ledger, reads the clause aloud, does not raise her voice, does not leave

kit.expression: The ledger itself is the instrument — every entry dated, witnessed, and cross-referenced to the cooperative's master roll. She can produce the binding obligation in writing on the spot (bind), and if a party disputes a figure she can walk the arithmetic aloud, line by line, until the error or the truth surfaces (reveal). No magic. Just ink, memory, and early-spring light through a forge doorway.

arrivalDetail.timing: World-day 15, just after first light, while frost is still on the south-lane cobbles
arrivalDetail.knock: Three knocks — brisk, flat, evenly spaced. Business knock. Not a friend's knock.
arrivalDetail.openingWords: "Bette Harrow, upvalley cooperative. I need three minutes with whoever holds the forge accounts. I know the hour."

provenance.hint: an unknown visitor at the south lane of Pell's forge — brisk knock, not Corvin or Aldric, someone with business or urgen
provenance.why: three knocks at the forge door with no established identity — the world pressing in on the expedition's departure

pip-cotter-mara-s-errand-runner.name: Pip Cotter, Mara's Errand-Runner
pip-cotter-mara-s-errand-runner.role: Child courier and small-task helper for Mara Wells
pip-cotter-mara-s-errand-runner.appearance: Seven years old, possibly eight — she is unsure and will tell you both ages depending on which she remembers. Wears an oversized blue wool coat that was someone else's first, the cuffs rolled four times and still dragging. Cheeks red from the cold and the running. Clutches whatever she's carrying in both hands like it is the most important object in the world, because Mara said so.
pip-cotter-mara-s-errand-runner.voiceHints: Breathless sentences, pauses to swallow air mid-thought. Repeats Mara's instructions almost verbatim before adding her own opinions uninvited. Calls adults by full name because that is what she has heard done. Takes promises seriously and remembers them exactly.

kit.move: Knows every shortcut between Millbrook's lanes and garden gates — the gap in the Cotter fence, the stepping-stones behind the mill, the path along the east ditch that stays dry into spring.
kit.reveal: Absorbs overheard details without meaning to and repeats them with complete accuracy when she trusts the listener, or when she forgets to be careful.

knowledge: The contents of the cloth Mara sent — she memorized Mara's words about it before she left, in case the cloth got wet
Every house in Millbrook that keeps a dog and which ones bite
That Mara has been up before dawn three mornings running — Pip noticed because she was also up, feeding the goat
Which neighbor children are sick this season and which are not — valley gossip absorbed from doorstep conversations while waiting to be let in
pip-cotter-mara-s-errand-runner.wants: To complete the errand correctly, catch her breath, and be given a small task on the return trip so the journey feels useful twice.
pip-cotter-mara-s-errand-runner.fears: Losing or ruining something she was trusted to carry. Being told she arrived too late.

reactsToReputation.trusted-by-mara: opens up immediately, will answer two questions for every one asked
reactsToReputation.unkind-to-children: goes quiet, delivers the message in the fewest possible words, leaves fast

pip-cotter-mara-s-errand-runner.notes: Not yet aware that she is a reliable source of low-level local intelligence. Mara is aware. Pip is loyal to Mara first and will report any strange treatment back to her without being asked.

provenance.hint: a girl of seven or eight in an oversized blue wool coat — Mara's errand-runner, practical and out of breath
provenance.why: The child messenger has just arrived in-scene with a cloth from Mara and needs a stable identity for future encounters

calvar.name: Calvar
calvar.role: Pre-Transition filtration engineer, author of the seventh-band monitoring specification — a man who stopped moving fifteen years ago because the light in one room told him everything he still needed to know
calvar.physicalDetail: Late fifties, though early-spring morning light makes him look older — the kind of age that comes from stillness, not hardship. Hands still precise. Keeps the window above the cooperage angled so the first plateau light crosses his drafting surface at exactly the seventh-band frequency. He has calibrated this by hand, yearly, for fifteen years.
calvar.voiceHints: Unhurried in a way that is not rudeness — he simply will not be rushed past the part that matters. Speaks about light the way other engineers speak about load-bearing calculations: not reverently, but exactly. When he senses evasion he goes very quiet and waits, and the waiting is harder than argument. Will not leave a sentence technically imprecise even when it costs him emotionally.

abilities.reveal: Calvar reads spectral signatures in ambient light the way others read handwriting — he can identify filtration drift, contamination band, or a falsified purity reading from the quality of illumination in a room. This is not mysticism; it is fifteen years of sitting still with the seventh band until it became a language.
abilities.bind: The seventh-band specification is a living instrument to him. He knows every node it touches, every engineer who trained under it, every deviation that has ever been logged. Persuading him is not a social act — it is a technical one. Give him a true reading and he is bound to it. Give him a false one and you have lost him entirely.

knowledge: The seventh-band monitoring specification in its entirety, including three unpublished addenda he chose not to file
What the diagram he left with Leth three days ago means — and what it does not mean, which is the more important half
That the seventh-band readings have been drifting in a direction that has no name in the pre-Transition literature, and has had no name for eleven months

The cooperage below him has been storing something other than barrels for two seasons; he has not reported it because the light it emits is not dangerous — it is, in a way he cannot yet specify, attentive
That leaving the room means losing the calibration, and that losing the calibration means the specification becomes a document rather than a living thing
calvar.wants: To be given a reason to leave that is technically sound — not an appeal to safety, not an appeal to duty, but a reading he cannot take from this window. He will go if the data requires it. He will not go for sentiment.
calvar.fears: That the drift in the seventh band is not contamination but communication, and that moving before he understands it will mean the message arrives and no one is left who can read it.

reactsToReputation.truthful: he opens the second notebook, the one he has not shown anyone
reactsToReputation.technically_credible: he treats you as a colleague and the conversation shifts from persuasion to collaboration
reactsToReputation.evasive_or_political: he answers every question accurately and volunteers nothing; the diagram becomes the whole of what you get

calvar.relationshipToLeth: Trusts Leth's judgment about people, not about urgency. The diagram was a gift, not an alarm — he expected Leth to sit with it for a month, not three days.
calvar.currentState: Early spring has shifted the morning angle two degrees north. He has been recalibrating since first light. The seventh-band reading this morning was the clearest it has been in eleven months, and he is not certain whether this means the drift is resolving or peaking.

provenance.hint: Calvar — pre-Transition filtration engineer, late fifties or older, living above the cooperage on the east lane of the E
provenance.why: Named by Leth as the engineer the party must find and persuade to leave the Edge District; the party will go to him next

offscreen.note: Reviewing last night's seventh-band log, Calvar found a seventeen-minute gap in the low-frequency baseline that his specification explicitly flags as instrument drift — but the gap's edges are too clean, too symmetrical, and he has spent the morning re-running the check three times rather than recording it as error.

Calvar ran a manual recalibration of the seventh-band sensor overnight and found a three-point drift he cannot yet attribute to instrument error or source change — he has written nothing down, but he has not looked away from the window.

vash.name: Vash
vash.role: Piecework lens-grinder of the Edge District — the one whose wheel was turning before the district woke, and who understands the array's optical layer the way water understands a crack
vash.voiceHints: Unhurried. Speaks in the cadence of someone who has spent years listening to the pitch of a wheel and knows that rushing ruins the curve. Does not perform ignorance but does not announce knowledge either — waits to see whether you are asking the real question or the surface one. When she decides you mean it, she will tell you exactly what she heard, what she saw, and what she made of it, in that order, without decoration.
knowledge: The fresher notation on the diagram — she heard Calvar speak it aloud, half to himself, while waiting for Leth's door; she did not know what it meant then and has been turning it over since
The optical layer of the array at a depth the weavers who commission her work do not suspect: she grinds to tolerances they specify without understanding why, and she has come to understand why
Which lenses in the eastern relay cluster were reground on non-standard prescription, and whose chit authorized it — she kept the chit because the prescription was wrong in an interesting way
That Leth's archive door carries sound badly, and that the east room is the worst-kept secret on the row
vash.wants: To understand what she overheard well enough to know whether she should have said something sooner. She is not afraid of consequence — she is afraid of having held a piece of something important and not known what it was a piece of.
vash.fears: Being made into a witness for something she only half-understood. That the half she missed is the half that matters.
vash.physicalDetail: Fine-grained abrasive dust has settled permanently into the creases of her hands and the inner corners of her eyes. In early-spring light, standing near a window, she looks briefly like she is made of the same material she works.
vash.dailyPattern: At the wheel before first light. Done by mid-morning. Afternoons she is difficult to find — she walks the valley rim, which the district assumes is idleness and which is not.

reactsToReputation.curious: she meets curiosity with curiosity; will ask what you already know before she adds to it
reactsToReputation.authoritative: goes quiet and literal; answers only exactly what is asked, nothing adjacent
reactsToReputation.truthful: opens — will produce the chit; will tell you what she heard word for word including her uncertainty about one syllable
reactsToReputation.threatening: does not flinch, does not flee; looks at you with the patience of someone who has learned that pressure applied wrong ruins the work

provenance.hint: Vash — a lens-grinder in the Edge District, east room of the same row as Leth's archive, does piecework for the optics w
provenance.why: Leth has just named Vash as the person who was on the grinding wheel the morning Calvar came — potentially the overhear

offscreen.note: While dressing her wheel's edge before first light, Vash reconstructed the overheard phrase word by word and realized the term she couldn't place was a pre-Transition calibration interval — specific enough that whoever said it had read the same foundational documents the array's optical layer was built from.

Vash paused her wheel mid-grind this morning and spent an hour reworking the fragment she overheard into a sequence of optical terms she does know, arriving at an arrangement that almost coheres — close enough that her uncertainty has sharpened from fog into a specific question.

dav-cutter.name: Dav Cutter
dav-cutter.role: Cut-cord relay watcher for Grael's network — posted to the south cut-lane behind the cooperage barrel-yard, reading arrivals and passing word
dav-cutter.voiceHints: Young enough that his stillness costs him something — he practices it. Speaks rarely and in pieces: a half-sentence, a direction of the chin, a breath held too long. Valley-grain runs through him despite himself; he reads the quality of silence the way a lay-brother reads scripture, and he knows when a light has changed before he can say how. Hand never leaves the left lapel of his coat. When Silas came through the gate, Dav felt it like a shifted candle-flame — and has not yet decided what the feeling means.
knowledge: The relay hand-signs Grael's cutcords use along the south cooperage lane — three variations for stranger, known, and known-and-wrong
The rhythm of the barrel-yard: which hours the coopers go quiet, where the gate shadow falls in early-spring morning light
That Silas came through — the shape of his arrival, not yet its meaning
The Low Lamp's back-passage geography well enough to be gone before a question becomes a confrontation
That the valley's stillness is not emptiness — it is attention, and he has learned, uneasily, to trust that
dav-cutter.wants: To pass the word clean and be done — no interpretation, no consequence, just the relay completed and the coin earned. He is quietly afraid that this particular message will not let him stay that simple.
dav-cutter.fears: That the valley's light will eventually make him legible — that something in the stillness here will read him back the way he reads arrivals, and that what it finds will cost him the cord.

reactsToReputation.network_known: relaxes one degree, does not show it, confirms the relay sign
reactsToReputation.outsider: goes mannequin-still, waits for the person to leave his lane
reactsToReputation.spiritually_attuned: unsettles him; he does not know why they keep looking slightly past him, and it makes him want to move
reactsToReputation.threatening: already gone — he was never quite here

abilities.name: Cut-Lane Read
abilities.description: Dav processes arrivals through the gate against the quality of early-spring morning light — the angle of a shadow, a breath misting wrong, a coat worn heavy on one side. He does not know he is doing it spiritually. He thinks it is just attention.

abilities.name: Relay Sign
abilities.description: Passes encoded hand-signal up the network without voice or paper — stranger, known, or known-and-wrong. The message moves; he stays. The valley stillness carries it faster than it should.

abilities.name: Cord-Cut Fade
abilities.description: Dav can vacate a lane so cleanly that witnesses reconstruct him out of ambient detail — a barrel stave, a shadow, the smell of new wood. In the valley's strong light, this costs him more than it used to. The light keeps finding edges.

provenance.hint: A relay watcher — young, cut-cord pack, hand near his coat, Grael's network — standing in the cut-lane south of the coop
provenance.why: A second watcher posted to cover the cooperage's rear exit, distinct from the fixed watcher on the street face — the par

siol-elven-traveler-at-the-hub-plaza-tal.name: Siol — Elven traveler at the Hub plaza, tall, pale coat, bir
siol-elven-traveler-at-the-hub-plaza-tal.fears: being undone by the very grain of this place
siol-elven-traveler-at-the-hub-plaza-tal.wants: to go on as they are, in a world that won't quite let them

provenance.hint: Siol — Elven traveler at the Hub plaza, tall, pale coat, birch-quiet stillness, named Huginn as Marrow without being tol
provenance.why: First genuine named encounter in the Hub — needs a stable identity so she can recur and the Steadwood passage can be a t

maren-voss.name: Maren Voss
maren-voss.role: Liminal fighter who crosses thresholds to win — patient at every gate, lethal the moment one opens
maren-voss.voiceHints: Speaks the way someone waits — fully, without fidgeting. Grey coat, collar up against the early-spring cold she doesn't seem to feel. Answers questions a beat after you expect, not because she's slow but because she's already past your question and choosing which part of the answer is worth giving. Never names her companions first; lets them step through on their own terms.
knowledge: The Waygate's threshold-logic — which passages open early, which resist, and what the valley asks before it lets you through
How to read a bout before it starts: the other fighter's weight on their threshold moment, when they commit, the half-step that gives everything away
That the Coliseum far arc rewards those who make their opponent cross into unfavourable ground — and she has walked every arc of it in her mind

companions.name: Aldric
companions.role: First second — manages her between bouts, reads the crowd for information she won't bother with
companions.note: Older, steady, doesn't smile much; has been at this long enough that his calm is the real kind

companions.name: Pell
companions.role: Second second — young enough to be nervous, sharp enough to be useful; scouts ground and brings her water without being asked
companions.note: Talks when Aldric doesn't, which means the two of them together cover everything

abilities.name: Threshold Step
abilities.description: Maren reads the liminal beat of any exchange — the half-moment a gate swings — and steps through it before her opponent has committed. She does not dodge; she is simply no longer where the blow was aimed, and she is already at the next threshold.

abilities.name: Gate Hold
abilities.description: She can stall a passage — freeze the moment of transition for herself or a companion — holding a threshold open or shut long enough to change the shape of a fight. Used defensively, it is a ward; used offensively, it pins an opponent mid-step.

abilities.name: Far Arc Patience
abilities.description: In the Coliseum's far arc, where sight lines are long and the crowd loses track, Maren goes still enough to become part of the threshold itself — then crosses the distance in a single passage-movement that reads as arrival, not approach.

maren-voss.wants: One clean bout at the far arc, properly offered. She has been at the Waygate since the first thaw, waiting for her name to come up through the right mouth. She does not want shortcuts; she wants the gate to open the right way.
maren-voss.fears: That she will be read as ordinary before the bout begins — that whoever faces her will arrive with a fixed idea of what a grey-coat threshold-walker does, and she will have to spend the whole fight correcting it instead of fighting.

reactsToReputation.patient: a small, real nod — she recognises her own quality in someone else
reactsToReputation.reckless: watches them the way you watch an open gate in a high wind: not afraid, not dismissive, just careful
reactsToReputation.Silas: she has heard the name from Orvil this morning; she has not said anything about it yet, but Aldric noticed she stood up straighter

provenance.hint: a threshold-walker in a grey coat — young, patient, waiting for a bout at the Coliseum far arc, with two companions as s
provenance.why: Orvil named them as Silas's potential opponent this morning — they need a stable identity and craft before the bout can

tane-solr.name: Tane Solr
tane-solr.role: Threshold-walker and named bout opponent — a man who has crossed the center-line enough times to stop counting and started watching the sand instead
tane-solr.voiceHints: Unhurried in a way that is not laziness but arithmetic — he has already run the numbers on you and does not need to rush. Speaks in short declaratives with long pauses between them, as if he is giving the words time to land before he adds more weight. Calls the sand 'the floor' the way carpenters call wood 'the material.' Does not brag. Does not diminish. Names things exactly. When he says your name he means it.
tane-solr.appearance: Lean in the way the valley's early-spring wind is lean — nothing wasted, everything purposeful. Grey coat, collarless, worn to the colour of old flint at the cuffs. Two seconds coiled at his left hip, their grip-wraps re-done so many times the cord beneath has fused into something that is no longer rope and not yet bone. Dawn finds him standing at the far arc before the torches are lit, not warming up, just watching the center-line as if it owes him a conversation.
knowledge: The Waygate Coliseum's far arc — every degree of angle the pre-dawn light hits the sand, and what it does to a fighter's depth perception
How the valley's threshold law reads a bout: where the line is, what crossing it costs, and the three ways experienced fighters use the cost as a weapon
The particular grammar of two seconds — reach, wrap, redirect, the half-breath of slack that telegraphs everything if you know to listen for it
Silas's name, and therefore something about Silas — enough to have agreed to the bout, not enough to have made up his mind
tane-solr.wants: A bout that is true — not won or lost but honest, where both fighters come to the center-line having brought everything they actually are and no more.
tane-solr.fears: That he has crossed so many thresholds he no longer feels them, and that the day he stops feeling the line underfoot is the day he becomes dangerous for the wrong reasons.
tane-solr.history: Tane came up through the valley circuit in a leaner season, when the Coliseum ran bouts by firelight because they could not afford dawn slots. He lost his first twelve named matches and won the thirteenth by not moving when every instinct in the crowd — and his opponent — was certain he would. He has been studying stillness as a weapon ever since. The grey coat was his instructor's; she died between seasons, not on the sand, and he has not replaced it. He is not sentimental about this. He is precise about it.

reactsToReputation.experienced: a single nod — peer-recognition, no ceremony
reactsToReputation.reckless: watches more carefully, says less, waits for the recklessness to teach the lesson he will not deliver in words
reactsToReputation.seeking: will speak plainly, once, about what the center-line actually is — then stops, because the rest has to be walked
reactsToReputation.named_opponent: meets their eyes at the far arc before dawn and lets the sand do the introduction

provenance.hint: Tane Solr — a lean threshold-walker in a grey coat, unhurried, experienced on the sand, waiting with two seconds at the
provenance.why: He is now Silas's named bout opponent at the center-line and will need a stable identity, personality, and history for t

offscreen.note: Tane spent the early part of the day watching two apprentices spar and said nothing, but afterward he smoothed the sand at the center-line himself, a thing he normally leaves to the groundskeepers.

cassiel-ord.name: Cassiel Ord
cassiel-ord.role: Former march-warden who walked away from the post and sat down in the bowl and has not decided what comes next
cassiel-ord.voiceHints: Economy of words born from years of writing terse post-reports nobody acted on. Does not explain himself unless pushed, and when pushed gives one careful sentence more than expected and then stops. The silence after he speaks is not unfriendly — it is the silence of someone who has learned that most follow-up questions answer themselves if you wait. Stillwater grain: surface calm, something held under it that has not been named yet.
knowledge: The March in the years bracketing Veth's posting — patrol routes, seasonal flood-lines, which threshold markers are still true and which have drifted
Why the Veth post was dissolved, or at least the version of it he was given, and the part of it he does not believe
The Raven's Home threshold: he has crossed it twice, knows what it costs, knows what it gives back, and will not say more than that without trust he has not granted yet
Early-spring snowmelt read from limestone and sky — when a valley like this will sheet-flood and how much warning you actually get
The name of whoever ordered the dissolution and what that person's interests are in the March going unwatched
cassiel-ord.wants: To sit here long enough to know whether what he is feeling is conscience or exhaustion — and to answer that honestly before he moves again.
cassiel-ord.fears: That the dissolution was not incompetence but intent, and that knowing it changes what he is obligated to do with the rest of his life.

reactsToReputation.warden_or_posted: goes very still, then asks one quiet question about where and when before he decides anything
reactsToReputation.connected_to_veth: trusts nothing, offers nothing, watches everything — but does not leave
reactsToReputation.civilian_or_outsider: slightly more open; the professional wariness does not apply and he is tired of it anyway
reactsToReputation.trustworthy_established: speaks in longer sentences, eventually stops editing himself

cassiel-ord.appearance: A lean man somewhere in his middle forties, the kind of lean that is not hunger but habit — he has been moving through difficult ground for a long time and his body organized itself around that. March-warden coat, wool, the grey-green of limestone shadow, one brass closure replaced with knotted cord. Boots still muddy from the threshold. He is sitting with his back to the outcrop and his knees drawn up, not sleeping — just not moving. His face is weather-seamed and unhurried, the expression of someone who has decided to be still until he is ready not to be. There is a short-handled field axe laid flat beside him, not gripped, not put away.
cassiel-ord.domainsNote: Derived from this NPC's authored spectrum against the great circle (SNG-174). Kind and domain are independent: a people tends toward positions, an individual holds their own.
cassiel-ord.functionsNote: Reveal: carries March knowledge the players cannot otherwise access cleanly. Bind: his loyalty to the post's dissolution-secret creates a tension that hooks the scene forward. Conceal: he is actively withholding the threshold-crossing account until trust is established — the concealment is the scene's pressure valve.
cassiel-ord.inGrainNote: Stillwater's Trouble grain: peace + falsehood — the bowl is quiet and the quiet is not honest. Cassiel sits in both registers: genuinely still, genuinely not ready to tell the truth. Early-spring sets the mood — snowmelt, limestone cold, the world pretending winter is done.

provenance.hint: A figure sitting against the limestone outcrop at the far edge of Stillwater's Trouble — someone who walked here from th
provenance.why: The boot-tracks that ended at the Raven's Home threshold belong to someone, and that someone is sitting in the bowl righ

the-low-lamp-inn.name: The Low Lamp Inn
the-low-lamp-inn.descriptionSeed: Tucked into the lower tier of the Edge District where the light-guide streets run closest to the valley floor, the Low Lamp sits over a junction conduit — the glow rises through pale stone floors and fills the common room from below, warm and directionless as afternoon through cloud. The building is two storeys of dressed sandstone with deep-set windows fitted with diffusion panels so the light inside is never harsh. The beds are thick-mattressed, the bath-room fed by a cistern the owner keeps properly heated, and the linens smell of cedar oil. Maret Doss, who runs it, is a compact woman of sixty-odd years whose conversational range does not include your name, your business, where you've come from, or where you're going. She does ask whether you'd prefer the bath before or after supper, because the water pressure is better before the evening crowd. She has been here since before the last treaty renegotiation and expects to be here after the next. The common room is quiet enough to think in, which is unusual for the Edge District and not accidental.
the-low-lamp-inn.encounterFlavor: A common room lit gold-from-below where two or three guests eat separately and don't introduce themselves; early-spring rain ticking against diffusion panels; the smell of cedar, hot water, and whatever Maret is cooking that isn't your concern unless you'd like a bowl. Occasionally a figure comes in from the cold, exchanges a look with Maret, goes upstairs, and is not there in the morning.
questSeeds: A guest in the corner room has been here eleven nights and hasn't come down today — Maret won't say how she knows something is wrong, only that she'd like it handled quietly and without the District wardens

The light-guide conduit under the inn has been cycling strangely at the third hour of the night, waking guests with a pulse of light brighter than it should be — someone with knowledge of Radiant infrastructure could trace it, if they were careful about who found out they were looking

A sealed letter was left with Maret for a guest who checked in under a name that matches someone Silas or Pell have reason to recognize — Maret will not confirm or deny the guest exists, but she hasn't burned the letter

Maret's cedar oil supplier hasn't come up from the valley floor in three weeks; she mentions this once, flatly, and doesn't mention it again — but the route he travels crosses ground that has been quietly problematic

provenance.hint: a modest but genuinely comfortable inn in the Edge District of the Radiant Plateau — warm, lit from below by the light-g
provenance.why: Silas and Pell need a named, returnable inn in the Edge District to spend the night — it will be a place they may return

the-attended-end.name: The Attended End
the-attended-end.description: Marrow tells you plainly what is ending here and how long it has — a person, a truce, a building, a road. It is never wrong and it never softens it.
the-attended-end.notFor: Preventing the ending; being asked to be gentle about it; anything that is not, in fact, ending.

chronicleCache.hash: d5|bpell:romantic:together|c|q|L10|You took the short way, and the short way was wr
chronicleCache.text: You have become the man who took the short way and found it was the only way — the path back gone, the light wrong, and something in you already changed before you understood what had changed you. You talked Calvar out of the Edge District, pulled him away from his filtration schematics and his seventh-band specifications and set him walking toward Millbrook to say what he knew about Grael's reign, and you did the same with Fendt, who carried his testimony in his own hands, the falsified ledger thread no longer just evidence but a wound walking upright. You fed a village — a dressed bull and sow carried down from the north wood — and you raised a cleaned rabbit in the upper meadow with no ritual and no preparation, just your hands and whatever the valley keeps in its old grain, which answered. At Pell Marsh's forge you named the death-bound spear Memory at dawn after a night of wine and repair, her maker's mark seated into the blade and Huginn watching at the stone ring; you kissed her at the forge bench while the light was still coming on, and that word, that deed, that mouth — all of it holds. You are bound now: to Pell, to Calvar who trusted you, to Aldric and Fendt and the rest of them; the spear carries your death-runes and a name that stuck, and wherever the hillside door leads, you are carrying all of that through it.

sessions.recap: Day seventeen passed through Silas Weir like weather through an open window — present, felt, and gone without leaving a mark anyone could name. No blood on his hands, no new doors opened or closed, no word carried to ears that needed it. He existed inside the hours, which is its own kind of labor, the kind that leaves no scar but slowly shapes the bone beneath. The world held its breath, and Silas held his alongside it.

Day eighteen, and Silas Weir moved like a man who'd finally stopped fighting the shape of what he was. On the road he disarmed three opportunists without breaking bones, read the grief sitting behind the broad man's eyes, named it plainly, pressed provisions into their hands, and sent them toward something worth doing — mercy given as instruction, not sentiment. Then he brought the hard people together: Dara, Aldric, Fendt, Mara, Calvar, all of them in the heat of Pell's forge with no exits easy and no pretense left, and in that single evening he turned a valley's quiet panic into four working threads — engineering, logistics, supply, accounts — each with a named lead and a direction to face. The crisis didn't end, but it stopped being formless, and that was his doing.

gm-road-opportunists-at-the-hawth.name: Road Opportunists at the Hawthorn Bend
gm-road-opportunists-at-the-hawth.setup: Two tired road-workers turned brigands have boxed the party at a narrow limestone bend — the broad one with a hatchet and the lean one with an iron-shod crook, both close enough that the ground forecloses easy retreat. Huginn is already above them.

personalArc.name: The Door That Closed Behind
personalArc.premise: A wright who finished something — completely, without pause, without asking if he should — discovers that the thin place was never an entrance. It was the consequence: the world sealed what he made into itself, and him with it, and now what he finished is alive somewhere ahead.
personalArc.stakes: To understand what he completed, and whether completion was the same as creation — or whether he built a door from the inside and never knew it.

legendNpc.name: The Finished Thing
legendNpc.role: the work he completed, now loose in the substrate of the world, half-made in his image and half-made in nothing's

stages.objective: the wrong angle of light — the thin place speaks in the grammar of his craft; read what it remembers of the making, and what it has already unmade since
the exilers were not entirely wrong — find who sent the warning before the council moved, and hear what they saw the work do in the last hour before it finished
what completion costs — the finished thing surfaces, wearing the logic of his hand, and asks to be read; what he made, he must now answer for

routes.author: claim it — name what the finished thing is, take the consequence of having made it, and write the last word the work was missing: himself
routes.unwrite: finish it again, differently — use the craft that built it to unmake it properly this time, and accept that the door closing behind him was always the point
routes.read: refuse both poles — read the work as it asked to be read, let it be what it finished into, and walk forward through the consequence as its maker, not its god
